artworks.description:
The artist plays the protagonist, Desyphus, a “perpetual commuter” on an adventure on the Circle Line. The videos projected on the wall below the station’s water-filled glass skylight take commuters through themes of travel, time, memory, and the train line. 

Fun Fact:
Every night at 7:29pm, Bras Basah transforms into an underwater video adventure starring artist Tan Kai Syng as a “perpetual commuter”! Ride the 41-metre escalator and you’ll spot a dreamy film full of riddles inspired by Alice in Wonderland and famous philosophers.

There are twenty-nine different videos that never play the same way twice — all projected beneath a giant glass skylight filled with water! The show only runs at night, when the sun isn’t too bright to spoil the magic.

The Amazing Neverending Underwater Adventures! by Tan Kai Syng at CC2 Bras Basah.

artworks.description:
The thirty-six white gypsum sculptures of Man and Environment are spread evenly across three pillars, depicting things such as rocks, plants, textures and wall cracks. These enlarged depictions encourage commuters to be more aware of the little details in their surroundings that they fail to notice during their commute. 

Fun Fact:
Look closely at the three white pillars – they’re not just decorations! Artist Baet Yeok Kuan turned tiny everyday details we often ignore – like wall cracks, floor scratches, and bumpy textures from plants and rocks – into big, bold sculptures!

He made them by pressing clay or doing rubbings of real objects found around the area. It's like turning a scratch on the floor into a superpower! Next time you’re at the station, see if you can spot a cable or crack hiding in the art.

artworks.description:
This colourful artwork depicts the daily movement of people. With arrows and shapes in multiple colours, the piece captures the dynamism, vibrancy and diversity in the MacPherson neighbourhood, with Singapore’s major ethnic groups represented by the colours.

Fun Fact:
This colourful artwork may look digital, but it started as a hand-drawn A3 sketch coloured for five hours a day — just to avoid even a single white dot! Artist Kay Kok used rulers, set-squares, and even a T-square to get every line perfect. The work was originally meant for Tai Seng station, but was swapped last minute because the other artwork was too heavy for MacPherson’s Civil Defence design.

And those four arrows? They're coloured after Singapore’s ethnic groups — but the glowing red rectangles? They’re actually meant to represent the MRT network making it all happen.

artworks.description:
Two colourful displays of equilateral triangles reflect off one another to metaphorically explore the relationship between commuters in the hustle of modern life. This richly-hued expression of beautiful ordered chaos also represents the organic and dynamic movements of the business and industries in the area.

Fun Fact:
Ever noticed the escalators at Tai Seng form a perfect triangle? That little detail sparked this whole artwork! Artist Francis Ng created three giant coloured panels — red, blue, yellow — that reflect and overlap to show “possibilities and probabilities". He switched from heavy glass to layered acrylic after safety rules kicked in, and called the final result a “beautiful sense of order and chaos". It's like a giant geometry daydream hidden in plain sight!

artworks.description:
Coins were used to pay for public transport in the past and are still used today at ticketing machines. This striking canvas of 164,800 one-cent coins showcases Singapore’s visual heritage through the tiny Vanda Miss Joaquim orchids on these out-of-circulation pieces.

The varying tones and depths of copper reflect the different journeys that each coin has taken.

Fun Fact:
Artist Jane Lee’s artwork uses 164,800 one-cent coins — but her first batch got confiscated at customs in China because officials didn’t believe they were for art!

She had to borrow coins from friends to create a sample, and when her glass-sandwich idea failed, the team ended up drilling every panel by hand. If one cracked near the end? They had to start over! Every coin features a tiny Vanda Miss Joaquim or the Singapore coat of arms — a copper tribute to our past.

artworks.description:
The sweeping painting of bold colours and brushstrokes celebrates the development of Singapore from swamp to bustling city. The artist created a painting to express movement in change and nature, capturing the optimism, joy and vitality he felt for Singapore’s achievements.

Fun Fact:
Artist Sarkasi Said created the original 5×8.3 metre masterpiece using batik techniques, splashing coloured wax on thick denim over four months outdoors in Solo, Indonesia, because the colours needed sunlight to set!

artworks.description:
This work combines the nation’s history, culture, geography and major events into a colourful and tongue-in-cheek map. It is filled with familiar landmarks like the Singapore flyer, Changi Airport and HDB public housing estates and peppered with caricatures such as Merlion and Sang Nila Utama on a boat.

Fun Fact:
This playful map began life as a drawing on just three A4 sheets with a regular black ballpoint pen. It’s packed with 2007 headlines like “Integrated Resorts + Casino” and fun nods to real events — even a reference to Singapore’s search for sand alternatives!

And hidden among the landmarks? The Beach Thick-Knee, a bird picked because it’s “so rare in Singapore, it is considered extinct". With full creative freedom, the artists coloured every detail into a chaotic, candy-coloured time capsule.

artworks.description:
The densely packed murals depict the artist’s feelings about the mindless rush of commuters in a train station. The three murals depicting daily life, family and the heartlands with a whimsical twist – students fly to school on rockets and animal-headed executives are rushing to work in a surreal world.

Fun Fact:
Artist Soh Ee Shaun took nearly a year to overcome a mental block before creating these colourful murals! He wandered through Bishan’s streets for inspiration, then drew his wild scenes by hand on A3 paper — think rocket-riding students, animal-headed office workers, and scientists launching fighter jets.

If you look closely, you’ll spot the station’s real architects and builders hidden in the heartland — wearing yellow helmets as a quiet tribute.

artworks.description:
Three drawings are each made up of only a single continuous line. The line represents a train journey where one traverses between time and space – it not only connects the MRT stations but also serves as a record of time. The 5x8.4m piece above the ticketing machine even shows the blueprint of the station, giving commuters a peek behind the construction of the station.

Fun Fact:
This entire drawing was made with just one continuous line — no lifting the pen! Look closely and you’ll spot all kinds of hidden surprises: a sun, a singing bird, and even reminders like “Use Less Water".

Artist Joshua Yang included doodles made while watching TV and drew directly over the station’s original blueprints, which you can see above the ticketing machines.

artworks.description:
Memories make a place.

The artist used written recollections from people about their most memorable places in Singapore and created a textual record of Singapore’s changing landscape, presented as a topography of the station’s surroundings.

Fun Fact:
This mural is made up of real memories shared by people all over Singapore — more than 100 stories were collected through surveys and interviews! Artist Hazel Lim turned them into a word-map based on satellite images of the Thomson area, with the densest memories layered in overlapping text.

And here’s a fun trick: the artist placed the more humorous memories right at eye level so you’d notice them first. Go ahead — read the walls, you might just spot someone’s favourite hangout from years ago!

artworks.artist: Lam Hoi Lit; Chua Chye Teck
artworks.description:
Nature is brought onto the platform with a large mural inspired by the traditional technique of Chinese paper cutting. Paper cuttings of different varieties of plants, seeds, branches, fish, and rocks are complemented by a water feature inside the station, serving as a prelude for commuters journeying to the Botanic Gardens.

Fun Fact:
Before this mural became a lush wall of plants and fish, the artists hand-cut hundreds of tiny shapes — each one a leaf, seed, or scale — with nothing but paper and scissors. They created three different designs, but only one made it through the review process.

The final artwork is a swirl of layered forms, capturing the feeling of wading through a hidden forest pond filled with life.

artworks.description:
The artist roped in 300 members of the community to create this lively collection of lines and brushstrokes in a celebratory riot of colour. The art is meant to represent the “in-between spaces” where identity is formed – not by what is there, but what is in-between.

Fun Fact:
Artist Erzan Bin Adam didn’t paint a thing — 300 people from the community did! At a Sunday block party, residents (including the former MP!) drew only lines using red, blue, and yellow paint. Some queued up three times just to try each canvas. One sceptical art teacher even came back later to say, “Well done". Erzan layered their work into six colourful panels — a tribute to the idea that identity lives in the spaces between us.

artworks.description:
Holland Village’s vibrant everyday scenes are depicted in a style reminiscent of American Pop Art. The artist made drawings from photographs he took after spending a day in the neighbourhood to capture its vibe.

Fun Fact:
Before turning to art, Jeremy Sharma was the guitarist for local indie band Tiramisu! For this mural, he wandered Holland Village for one afternoon, took photos, and sketched nearly fifty scenes. One quirky addition? A figure from an airline safety card — chosen because it looked oddly serious but “kitsch and light". The artwork’s name, Holland Beat , reflects both the neighbourhood’s energy and his musical roots.

artworks.description:
Playing on the station’s name, which is Italian for “good view”, the artist used a photomontage technique to show a single 9m tall tree in the station. The reconstituted image represents how we usually view tall trees in parts, instead of as a whole.

Fun Fact:
This towering tree was inspired by artist Gilles Massot’s first impressions of Singapore in 1981 — not skyscrapers or Chinatown, but the lush jungle of South Buona Vista Road! He discovered a eucalyptus tree with peeling white bark that revealed a red trunk, which matched the station’s interior walls.

To show how we never see a whole tree at once, Gilles used a photomontage technique, breaking the tree into parts like a visual time-lapse. “Any moment", he says, “is always made of that time-perception.".

artworks.artworkTitle: A Visual Narrative of Pandemonic Rhythmic Movement
artworks.description:
This colourful triptych is a visual metaphor for life in the train station, part of a system that moves people around throughout the day. Representing the seemingly chaotic daily movement of commuters, the series of white lines over a pulsating background of colours captures the station’s dynamism.

Fun Fact:
Artist Yek Wong once called himself the “Barry White of post-conceptual painting” — and yes, he sees his artwork like a love song! His colourful, zig-zaggy triptych reflects how life in a train station can feel like beautiful chaos when you speed it up.

He made this piece entirely on his computer, fitting right in with the techy vibe of Fusionopolis. And his bold neon colours? Inspired by his years living in Las Vegas, where “whatever looks good is all that matters!”
captions:
A Visual Narrative of Pandemonic Rhythmic Movement by Yek Wong at CC23 one-north.

artworks.description:
This interactive art piece allows commuters to send a text message to Poetry Mix-Up and watch as a poem is generated by an algorithm and projected on a screen at the station’s lift shaft. Besides bringing back the art of writing poetry, the artist aims to encourage commuters to look up from their mobile screens and live in the moment.

Fun Fact:
Here, you can literally text a poem into existence! The system scans your message for mood and keywords, then remixes it with lines from over 20,000 classic poems, some written 200 years ago! There’s even a smart filter that catches bad words — in multiple languages.

The goal? To bring poetry back into public life . . . and maybe get a few people to look up from their phones.
captions:
Poetry Mix-Up by Mixed Reality Lab at CC24 Kent Ridge.

Photo courtesy of Land Transport Authority

artworks.description:
The artist used iconic landmarks in western Singapore and created an imaginary black-and-white vista against the grey backdrop of the station. Inspired by the area’s history as a swamp before it was reclaimed, the artist drew parallels with Haw Par Villa’s themes of mortality and reincarnation.

Fun Fact:
This dreamy black-and-white scene isn’t just fantasy — it’s packed with landmarks from western Singapore, all floating like memories in mist. Inspired by how this area was once a swamp (yes, really!), artist Tan Wee Lit mixed themes of change, rebirth, and Haw Par Villa’s famous stories into one surreal skyline.

Spot the tiger? That’s a tribute to the villa’s original mascot!

artworks.description:
This set of posters promotes an imaginary film on the birth and death of Lieutenant Adnan, a World War II hero who fought at the Battle of Pasir Panjang, which took place near the station. Commuters learn snippets of Lieutenant Adnan’s story through the posters spread across the station.

Fun Fact:
The posters you see here aren’t for a real movie — but they sure look like it! Artist Ho Tzu Nyen created a full-on fake film campaign about WWII hero Lieutenant Adnan, casting actor Aaron Aziz (who’s played Adnan before!) in moody scenes inspired by 16th-century painter Caravaggio.

The posters are spread across the station like scenes from a film: some tell his story, others zoom in like a movie reel. The twist? You become the director — piecing it together in your mind, one frame at a time.

artworks.description:
Donated by: PSA International
Year: 2022

The Bo-men transcend race and nationality, connected by the common thread of their shared humanity. Generally displayed in groups, through form and posture they convey a sense of being "alongside" each other, by striving together towards a shared goal. The sculptures celebrate human potential, resilience, and the spirit of collaboration, which echo themes central to the core ethos shared by PSA and Singapore.

Daisy Boman is a world-renowned artist who creates unique pieces that pay tribute to the shared bonds between individuals and their potential to create movements of purpose and passion towards a shared vision.
captions:
Stride Alongside the Bo-men by Daisy Boman at CC26 Pasir Panjang.
Donated by PSA International

artworks.artworkTitle: Without Which / Would Have Been / Impossible
artworks.description:
This ambiguous piece on the platform’s lift shaft comprises patterns of coloured dots that were inspired by the rocky shores of Labrador beach, together with its title, Without Which/ Would Have Been / Impossible . The artist meant for it to be open to each commuter’s interpretation, and aims to encourage them to think about things they do not understand.

Fun Fact:
This artwork came from a single rock picked up by the artist in the park’s rocky shore — then blown up to reveal its tiny textures! The patterns of dots you see are drawn from a microscopic view of that rock, symbolising how everything in nature is interconnected.

The mysterious words? They form a haiku, and the artist wants you to finish the sentence however you like. (“Without which . . . what?”) Artist Heman says it’s meant to feel incomplete — like stepping on uneven ground — just like the real rocky paths of Labrador Park.
captions:
Without Which / Would Have Been / Impossible by Heman Chong at CC27 Labrador Park.

artworks.artworkTitle: Notes Towards a Museum of Cooking Pot Bay
artworks.description:
The mind map of interconnected relationships offers a detailed yet nuanced understanding of Telok Blangah. Presented as a plan for a hypothetical community museum, the work draws connections between objects, buildings, places, people, and nature. It presents established facts with solid lines and unverified information with dotted ones.

Fun Fact:
Artist Michael Lee built a fake museum for Telok Blangah — complete with rooms like the “Cabinet of Curiosities", “Nowhere, Everywhere, Anywhere Room", and even “A Musement Park” for notable locals! He included real history (like where Sun Yat Sen once stayed), and future “predictions” like a crater on the Moon named after Telok Blangah in the year 2229 and a Transformers vs SpongeBob 5D movie premiering at VivoCity.

His goal? To make us rethink what’s real, what’s remembered, and how the wildest ideas might just belong in our museums, too.

Notes Towards a Museum of Cooking Pot Bay by Michael Lee at CC28 Telok Blangah.

artworks.description:
The artist captured the sounds and rhythm of everyday commuters and the train frequency as well as the volume of passengers coming into HarbourFront MRT station through a single turnstile. This data was translated visually for weekday and weekend traffic, alluding to the human rhythm at the interchange.

Fun Fact:
Artist Jason Ong turned real commuter data into wave-shaped sculptures! He sent two assistants to record train arrivals and passenger numbers through a single turnstile — one weekday, one weekend. Then he shaped that data into “fins” using phase shift (a music trick by Steve Reich), and arranged them into red waveforms on the wall.
captions:
Commuting Waves by Jason Ong at CC29 Harbourfront.

artworks.artist: Lim Mu Hue @ Lim Aik Lee
artworks.description:
The last and largest work by the late woodcut engraving artist, this black-and-white piece pays homage to theatre. Scenes of early settlers in Singapore enjoying shadow puppetry and other performances are presented across seven enlarged woodblock prints, which commuters will see while en route to the nearby Esplanade Theatres on the Bay. 

Fun Fact:
This dramatic black-and-white mural was the last and largest artwork by Singapore’s pioneer woodblock artist, Lim Mu Hue — a true love letter to theatre. But did you know he secretly drew himself into the artwork as one of the puppet carvers (look for the man with a moustache in striped pants)? He even travelled to China to study puppet makers up close!

Despite being diagnosed with cancer while working on the mural, he remained fiercely dedicated — with his whole family helping out, from emailing drafts to scanning woodcuts and making edits together.

The final mural blends Teochew opera, Javanese shadow puppets, and stories of old — a tribute to Singapore’s multicultural roots. Lim’s motto? “Life is like drama, drama comes from life . . . now let the show begin".
captions:
A Piece of Ice-Clear Heart by Lim Mu Hue @ Lim Aik Lee at CC3 Esplanade.
Photo: Darren Soh

artworks.description:
According to its creators, this installation is an “illuminated celebration of our collective dreams”. Suspended under a skylight over escalators, the droplets playfully reflect rays and create a moving kaleidoscope on the station floors. These waves of light mirror the ebb and flow of commuter traffic and reflect the interaction of passengers in transit with the art piece. 

Fun Fact:
Did you know Promenade station has secret “disco balls” hiding in the sky? Artist collective PHUNK created giant droplets that catch the sunlight and scatter rainbow patterns across the station! They even secretly named the artwork so the first letters spell DISCO — Dreams In Social Cosmic Odyssey! Next time you're there, spot the sparkles and imagine a hidden party happening right above you!

artworks.description:
Four large reliefs above the platform doors showcase overlooked wild plants that play a key role in holding soil together on reclaimed land, on which the station was built. The artwork gives a starring role to these unappreciated plants and aims to remind commuters of how there is value in everything if they just take a closer look. 

Fun Fact:
When artist Khiew Huey Chian was just nine years old, he trekked alone across this wild stretch of land on his way home from school – back then, it was empty, hot, and covered in scrappy little plants. Those very same plants inspired his station artwork decades later!

His art celebrates these overlooked wild plants that helped hold the land together before it became a station. He even wanted to line the whole platform with them – but had to trim it back because it might’ve blocked train cables! Turns out, weeds can be way cooler than we thought.
captions:
Re-Claiming The Peripherals by Khiew Huey Chian at CC5 Nicoll Highway.

artworks.description:
This series of floating human silhouettes represents the beauty, energy, and fluidity of the human body during sports. Grace, form and power are captured in flight to form a dynamic set piece reminiscent of a dance recital in the air.

Fun Fact:
Those flying silhouettes above you aren’t digital effects — they’re real photos! Artist Roy Zhang worked with a Brazilian model trained in capoeira (a martial art that’s part dance, part sport!) to capture the perfect jump mid-air — on a trampoline. He took hundreds of shots in one day, asking the model to imagine sports like football, rugby, and even wakeboarding.

The final images were chosen not to show off a famous athlete, but to represent anyone chasing their dreams.

Fun Fact:
Those flying silhouettes above you aren’t digital effects — they’re real photos! Artist Roy Zhang worked with a Brazilian model trained in capoeira (a martial art that’s part dance, part sport!) to capture the perfect jump mid-air — on a trampoline. He took hundreds of shots in one day, asking the model to imagine sports like football, rugby, and even wakeboarding.

The final images were chosen not to show off a famous athlete, but to represent anyone chasing their dreams.

The Perfect Moment by Roy Zhang at CC6 Stadium.

artworks.description:
Legend has it that British naval commander Lord Louis Mountbatten painted his ships “Mountbatten pink” during World War II – he claimed it was an effective camouflage at dawn and dusk. This tactic was never proven, but he survived the war and had a road next to this station named in his honour.

This photo series shows a modern ship during “pink” hours, paying tribute to the eccentric idea.

Fun Fact:
One of the ship photos at Mountbatten is totally fake — and that’s the point! Artist Jason Wee blended photography with full-on movie-level CGI, rendering a completely made-up pink ship that took three weeks to generate on a computer. The other photo is real — snapped just as the sun hit that sweet “pink hour". Together, they play with truth and fiction, just like the story of Lord Mountbatten's oddball warship paint job.

And if the pink feels extra intense? That’s because Jason designed it to glow like a dreamy “pink corridor” when the station lights hit just right.

artworks.description:
This artwork captures the colourful everyday life in the Dakota Crescent heartlands. The large art piece features household objects found inside and outside residents’ homes, along the HDB flat corridors and other shared spaces.

By recording these items, it also acts as a visible archive of the area.

Fun Fact:
The artists behind this piece went door to door in Dakota Crescent, collecting stories along with objects! One neighbour, Miss Ang, even showed them her fridge covered in magnets collected over fifty years. Inspired by her layout, they displayed all seventy items — including a tricycle and a lift door — at actual size.

And here’s a sneaky twist: the artists even snuck themselves into the artwork as newscasters on a mini TV!​

artworks.description:
The colourful and abstract pictographs are inspired by the evolution of the district from a rural village to a satellite town. It features icons related to different stages of Paya Lebar’s development, such as symbols of pigs in a pig sty, a community tap, a dhoby laundry man, the iconic post office complex, and aeroplanes landing and taking off.

Fun Fact:
This artwork is like a time machine made of stone! Artist Salleh Japar grew up nearby in the 1960s, and based many icons on real memories — like planes taking off from Paya Lebar Airport and picking edible leaves when he was hungry. One figure shows a man on a motorcycle, inspired by his dad’s old Norton bike.

Look closely — you’ll spot a lion’s head (a nod to Lion City Hotel) and sparkly stones chosen after two weekends of exploring the neighbourhood!

artworks.description:
The art piece celebrates Singapore’s rich history as a port of call, located on major trade routes in Southeast Asia. The mural is a collage of different drawings of ships by Singaporeans aged seven to twelve based on historical images but with a dose of fantasy and imagination. The work celebrates history and the hopes for a better future.

Fun Fact:
Over 1,000 ships were drawn by students aged seven to twelve for this artwork — from dragon-powered vessels to floating shopping malls! Artist Lee Wen ran workshops across fifteen schools, and picked the most imaginative ones (not the “prettiest”) to feature.

The mural’s title comes from a Bob Dylan song Lee loved — a tune about hope, justice, and dreams of a better world.

artworks.description:
Two giant-sized boys peek around public housing blocks, signifying a sense of curiosity and the spirit of adventure. The rows of tall blocks allude to the hilly landscape of Bukit Panjang, which means “long hills” in Malay.

Fun Fact:
Those two towering boys in the photo? They’re not models — they’re real kids from the neighbourhood! Artist John Clang photographed them with three cameras at once to capture different views of the same moment. The shot was taken on a rainy day (yes, that rain was real too), which John calls a symbol of “rebellious spirit".

And where’s that mysterious rooftop they’re standing on? It's actually a multi-storey car park near Block 541 — chosen because the bland buildings made the boys pop like giants watching over their hometown.

artworks.description:
As a nod to the nature and history in the Stevens and Bukit Timah area surrounding the station, this work highlights manually stamped patterns of the foliage, seeds, and fruits of nutmeg and rubber trees. These crops occupied the area back in the 19th century.

Fun Fact:
This station’s artwork was made using real rubber stamps — and guess who helped? Hundreds of students from the school across the street (Singapore Chinese Girls School)!

They stamped nutmeg fruits, rubber seeds, and tree bark patterns by hand. Some stamps went upside down, some were smudgy, but the artist loved the mistakes. It’s like a giant nature jigsaw made by happy accidents, and every dot, smudge, and stamp is part of a secret garden planted by the neighbourhood!

artworks.description:
This intricate piece illustrates an imagined Singapore in 2200. Reflecting our nation’s constant urge to progress and adapt to changes, the mural presents an alternate reality of Singapore’s landscape above ground and underwater.

Fun Fact:
At this station, the future is stacked sky-high — and deep underwater, too! Everything in the mural was drawn the old-school way: with a trusty pencil, no fancy computers.

Zoom in and you might find flying homes, floating parks, or even a tiny “YOU ARE HERE” sign waving at you. It’s like a giant game of " Where’s Wally" , but in a wild future-Singapore mash-up!

artworks.description:
The glimmering installation of geometric patterns made of aluminium was inspired by the patterns in traditional Indian saris. The tessellated triangular patterns also integrate the notion of weaving, interlacing the architecture and artwork to form a single entity.

Fun Fact:
If you look up, you might just spot a giant floating sari—made of metal! This shimmering artwork is stitched together from over 1,000 triangle pieces, inspired by the hidden meanings inside real saris. Some triangles form a lotus flower, others a fish or herringbone.

It's like a glowing puzzle of symbols, dancing in the air above your head — woven not with thread, but with geometry and light.

Students:
Andreas Schlegel, Betty Susiarjo, Chelsea Zhao Xin, Chen Shitong, Luke Heng, Ronald Cheah, Xiuting Yang

This bright green lift shaft features local vintage objects acquired from The Thieves’ Market against a backdrop that looks motherboard. The piece attempts to contrast the binary reflections of Singapore’s youths, who live in a world of gadgets and technology but are nostalgic for history and tradition.

Fun Fact:
Before this station existed, the area around Rochor was full of colourful flats, bustling shops, and hidden treasures. The LASALLE art students went on a mission to capture it all — sketching old phones, cassette tapes, teapots, and even quirky toys they discovered nearby.

Many of those places are gone now, but their drawings live on in the walls of the station — like friendly time-travellers inviting you to peek into old Singapore.

This interactive piece prompts commuters to pay closer attention to their surroundings and look up from their mobile devices to engage with art. Coloured discs hidden behind the glass panels can only be spotted from certain angles by those who are curious enough to look.

Fun Fact:
This artwork features five coloured discs strategically placed behind glass panels. As you move along the travellator, these discs play a game of hide-and-seek, appearing only from certain angles.

The artist collaborated with engineers to fine-tune the discs' placement, ensuring the illusion aligns perfectly with the travellator's speed. It's a subtle invitation to look up and engage with your surroundings during your commute.​

artworks.description:
The dynamic art piece examines the geology and traditions of Singapore, focusing on the layers of cultural sediments. Visually, it also reminds one of colourful local kuehs, shedding light on everything from Singapore’s multicultural food to traditions.

Fun Fact:
To make this underground artwork, the artist didn’t just dig through soil — she also munched her way through cake! Ana Prvački sampled layer after layer of kueh lapis, marble cake, and more, matching each dessert to real soil from beneath the station. So yes, this might be the only MRT art ever powered by a sweet tooth.

Her advice? “Let us eat cake while we can!”

artworks.description:
Inspired by bamboo leaves, the mosaic gradients depict the dense network of plant cells seen at the molecular level. This serves as a metaphor for the station as the heart of Downtown, with a multitude of businesses located around it.

Fun Fact:
These giant leaves aren’t here to be realistic — they’re here to mess with your eyes and your fingers! Step back and they look like falling leaves. Get close, and they pixel into abstract patterns like you’re zooming in with a microscope. The artist actually drew the whole design square-by-square on graph paper, then digitally blew it up to look like pixel art!

Best part? You’re allowed to touch it — feel the bumpy mosaic tiles and pretend you're solving a secret nature puzzle with your fingertips.

Telok Ayer – meaning “bay water” in Malay – was where Chinese immigrants first set foot in Singapore. This piece celebrates the area’s history with cheerful abstract forms inspired by plantations and water.

Fun Fact:
Telok Ayer station might look normal — but it’s actually an enchanted riverbed in disguise! Artist Lim Shing Ee imagined commuters as little swimmers flowing through colourful pebbles, squishy blobs, and giant stone-like columns. She even made a model of the whole station at home, like building a dream world in miniature.

If you feel extra floaty walking through — don’t worry, you’re just going with the flow.

artworks.description:
The everyday scene of laundry on a clothesline is given a creative twist with plenty of colour and an illusion of movement, as if the clothes are reacting to the flow of commuter traffic around the station.

Fun Fact:
At this station, laundry doesn’t just hang — it dances! The artist spent years turning everyday clothes into a parade of joyful colours that flutter down the wall like a laundry conga line. Look closely and you might spot socks, bedsheets, even a famous "Good Morning" towel flapping along.

Thanks to a special optical trick, the clothes seem to move as you walk by — so yes, this is probably the only time it’s polite to stare at laundry in public.

artworks.description:
This work pays homage to Singapore’s creative high-rise gardeners. It presents a collage of everyday items found within Singaporean homes, such as toilet brushes, arranged to form flowers and plants in picturesque gardens.

Fun Fact:
Those delicate garden-like patterns at Cashew? They’re made entirely from cleaning brushes! Artist Donna Ong sourced all shapes and sizes — toothbrush heads, bristle wands, even nail scrubbers — to build her dream-like landscape.

The result? A whimsical world made of brushes and imagination — and yes, she still has so many leftover brushes, she jokes about giving them away to visitors.

artworks.description:
Students:
Sheryl Law, Reena Ng, Tay Yuling, Tsai Chingwen, Ooi Qiyang, Poornima Bargotra, Sheetal Muralidhara

For this work, 800 original photographs of buildings, people, and objects were collected and used to create a collage of life-size student bodies. These forms lead commuters from the station to NAFA and Singapore Management University.

Fun Fact:
This station’s artwork took years—and a full-on school adventure — to complete! NAFA students kicked things off with a proposal, then launched a whole photo campaign, convinced people to submit images, and even handed out prizes to get the community involved. They had to squeeze in meetings between school and life, and some were already abroad when the final piece went up.

The result? A memory mosaic made of over 800 little snapshots, forming student-shaped silhouettes that now wave to commuters every day. It’s like a group project that became a time capsule for the neighbourhood.

Featuring images of vibrant scenes and items from the former Sungei Road Thieves' Market, this piece expresses the spirit and energy of the area and its community. The angled and juxtaposed images create a sense of personal journey and experience, similar to looking into a kaleidoscope.

Fun Fact:
Before this station existed, artist Lydia Wong used to visit the nearby Thieves' Market—not just to look, but to hunt for art supplies as a student! Years later, knowing the market’s days were numbered, she returned with her camera to capture its colourful characters and quirky charm.

One ice-cream uncle even teased her for looking so serious, calling her the “blur lady with a camera". Today, that same uncle appears in the station’s artwork — forever spinning through a kaleidoscope of old-school Singapore life.

artworks.description:
Impressions and memories of Bendemeer inspired the artist to create an artwork that celebrates patterns found in local fabrics and architectural motifs on the buildings found in the area.

Fun Fact:
This station’s artwork is a patchwork of memories — and amusement parks! The artist grew up in the 1960s near the legendary New World amusement park, where her grandfather worked.

Years later, while exploring Bendemeer, she stumbled across the old gate of New World, still standing near City Square Mall. That moment inspired her to stitch together a new kind of “world", made from flying footballs, butterflies, batik patterns, and fabric textures. Even the peony, hibiscus, and paisley symbols represent the neighbourhood’s mix of cultures — woven together like a beautiful, busy textile.

artworks.description:
This series of pictograms recreates daily sights and scenes, injecting them into the daily commute. From a bag of kopi-o to construction workers hard at work, these pictograms add fun and intrigue in unexpected places around the station.

Fun Fact:
This station hides a tiny army of magical “elves” — if you know where to look! Peek around and you’ll spot miniature pictogram workers: one painting walls, another lifting a pillar, and a cheeky crew of flies buzzing in the men's toilet.

The artist grew up in a ship repair workshop and filled the station with secret symbols of Singapore’s industrial soul, like kopi-o packets, birdcages, and cage lamps. One little guy even pushes a wheelbarrow to point you toward the exit. It’s like a treasure hunt of everyday heroes in safety boots.

artworks.description:
In a nod to the religious landscape around Mattar Road, the artist transformed agarwood shavings – a rare type of wood used in incense and perfumes – into a cosmic landscape. The work acts as a subtle wayfinding device, leading commuters to and from the platform and concourse.

Fun Fact:
This cosmic-looking artwork was made from slivers of incense wood with a heavenly scent! The artist discovered agarwood — sometimes called the “wood of the gods” — at a friend’s place, and the smell stuck with her.

Around Mattar, it’s used in all kinds of sacred rituals, so she photographed the pieces, blew them up huge, and floated them across the station like celestial messengers. One moment they look like meteorites . . . the next, like blessings from another dimension.

artworks.description:
This typographical installation captures the thoughts and sentiments of twenty-seven MacPherson residents who were interviewed during the National Day celebrations in 2013.

The artwork is rendered in Singapore’s four official languages: English, Chinese, Malay, and Tamil.

Fun Fact:
This artwork is basically a giant love letter from MacPherson residents! The artist and her team knocked on doors, chatted with aunties and uncles, and gathered stories — from favourite hawker stalls to sweet neighbourly moments like leaving spare keys “just in case".

She then stitched their words into a 34-metre-long mural made of towering quotes in all four national languages. One line rises above them all: “What Makes MacPherson Home Is the People". It’s not just a mural — it’s a voice wall for the whole neighbourhood.

artworks.description:
In Malay, "Ubi" refers to the tapioca plant. This artwork comprised of a stainless-steel sculpture and stainless-steel inlays onto a granite floor, representing the tuberous plant and an abstraction of its roots. The skeletal silhouette of the roots symbolises solidarity in Singapore's multiracial and multicultural society.

Fun Fact:
This shiny sculpture is inspired by something totally unexpected: tapioca roots! The artist grew up in Ubi when it was still a kampong, surrounded by tapioca plants that helped feed his family of twelve. Years later, he returned to those memories — of stubby roots and laundry starch — to design a floating web of stainless-steel lines that now hangs in the station.

Look out for the five extra tapioca parts hidden across the floor like secret ingredients from an old family recipe.

artworks.description:
The artist invited a diverse community of residents, workers and students around the station to write "Welcome to Kaki Bukit" while filming their every hand motion while writing. These handwritten messages create a dynamic digital welcome signage that greets commuters as they descend the escalators.

Fun Fact:
The welcome sign at this station isn’t printed or painted — it’s handwritten on video by people from the neighbourhood! Students from Maha Bodhi School wrote “Welcome", workers from the nearby industrial buildings wrote “Kaki Bukit", and the artist wrote the tiny word “to” in between, like a human bridge connecting both sides. Even the markers were carefully chosen so the ink would flow perfectly in the mobile writing booth he built!

Watch the screens closely — you never know when a hand might sneak in, wipe the words away, and start the whole message over again.

artworks.description:
The artist aims to harmonise the station with its environment by bringing nature, community, and history into the station atmosphere. The types of leaves were chosen from the trees surrounding the station.

Fun Fact:
This station’s leafy sculptures are actually built on shadows — not leaves! The artist photographed real Umbrella tree leaves until he noticed their shadows were way more interesting. He turned those shadowy outlines into floating bronze shapes that drift across the station like a slow-motion breeze.

Fun bonus? The six leaf sculptures are all just two designs cleverly rotated.

artworks.description:
This body of photographic work documents the remnants of the Malaysian KTM railway line that ran for the last time in June 2011. It captures the iconic structures that formed the rail line, such as the steel bridge near Hillview station and the railway tracks that were left behind.

Fun Fact:
Photographer Darren Soh once lugged a massive Hasselblad camera through a mosquito-filled forest at night — just to get the perfect shot of three old railway bridges! Why? Because he wanted commuters to feel like they were on the tracks themselves.

Some photos face the bridge, others show the view looking away — like snapshots of a journey now lost to time. And yes, these are the only site-specific remains of the KTM railway still near the MRT today.

artworks.description:
Inspired by the waterbody that the station is named after, this artwork represents the connections between the movement of water and the flow of human traffic through a station.

The work also includes a typographical installation on the lift shaft and a stylised flow bench.

Fun Fact:
This station’s artwork is a quiet reminder that big dreams can still ripple through your daily routine. Artist Yong Ng grew up near Bedok Reservoir and wanted to bring some of that calm, flowing energy into the station.

A hopeful quote trickles down the lift shaft like falling water, while raindrop shapes swirl across the floor and even form a curvy bench. It’s like a little signal from the reservoir itself: keep going; your dreams are just around the bend.

artworks.description:
The artist photographed more than 200 members of the community around the stations during a five-day public event. These photos were combined with images of landmarks and things from around the area to form a collage that reflects the energy and optimism of the neighbourhood.

Fun Fact:
If you spot someone fist-pumping on the platform wall, you’ve arrived at Tampines West! For this giant group photo collage, the artist asked residents to pose like local superheroes — some even wore festive clothes or their army uniforms.

Look closely and you’ll also find familiar landmarks like the spiral sculpture near Blk 936 and the community club tower, all arranged in a colourful burst that feels like a parade frozen mid-celebration.

artworks.artworkTitle: The Big Round & The Tall Long
artworks.description:
Comprising a white round structure and an orange-red long one, this set of large geometrical forms brings a sense of calm and order into the station environment.

The shapes on the wall are complemented by reflecting benches on the platform, creating a sense of symmetry.

Fun Fact:
These two massive shapes — the Big Round and the Tall Long — weren’t just dropped in for fun. The artists made them just big enough to feel like gentle giants, not monsters. One of them even “bounces” alongside the lift when you ride up or down!

They worked out every detail — from colours that match the station's design, to textured paint that pops off the wall — and even tested if commuters would feel different walking past. The result: two playful forms that feel alive, inviting, and perfectly at home in the station.

Fun Fact:
At Tampines East, everyone was told to raise their hands in a big cheerful greeting — so the whole station feels like a community flash mob frozen mid-woohoo! Artist Jing Quek stitched together dozens of portraits into one joyful mural, packed with familiar faces and neighbourhood pride.

Spot the old Tampines Junior College, a hidden clock tower, and enough smiles to brighten any Monday morning.

artworks.description:
The artist invited the students from Singapore University of Technology and Design (SUTD) to contribute their fingerprints and adjectives that describe their current selves, which made the artwork a time capsule capturing the self-ascribed identities of university students at a point in time before they embark on their adulthood. Presented in lenticular technology, the adjectives are embedded in their fingerprints, only to reveal themselves at specific angles as commuters view the piece.

Fun Fact:
This artwork was inspired by a childhood ruler. Yep, the kind where images “moved” when you tilted them! Artist Boo Junfeng remembered that little thrill and used the same lenticular magic to create this wall of fingerprints and floating words.

Hundreds of SUTD students lined up to take part — some laughed, others treated it like a secret diary. Their chosen words flash in and out of view as you walk by, like thoughts darting across a crowded mind.

artworks.description:
The artist meant for the two reflective isometric chairs and a bulbous speech balloon to not only signify the importance of organic communication in an era of technological advancement and digital media, but also the spirit of free trade. Having the surrounding colours of the station reflected in the artwork’s shiny surfaces transformed them into two sets of magnificent kaleidoscopes, which also symbolise cultural exchange.

Fun Fact:
This station has chairs too big to sit on and conversations you can’t quite hear. The artist imagined Expo as one giant dinner party — where people from all over the world meet, talk, and then vanish. The floating chairs and speech bubbles may look 3D, but they’re actually optical illusions made from flat steel!

Even the installers got tricked and cut two identical pieces, thinking they were mirrored. It’s a sculpture that listens more than it speaks — just like a great dinner guest.

artworks.description:
Featuring a mix of letterforms from Chinese, Tamil, Latin, and Jawi – layered upon one another – this work highlights multiculturalism and diversity. It invites commuters to view it as both art and writing and "hover" between reading and looking.

Fun Fact:
Boedi almost didn’t submit this idea — it came to him just days before the deadline! After a long creative block, he drew inspiration from old scripts, cave carvings, and calligraphy, finally submitting a “wordless” artwork made from over eight different writing systems, from Jawi and Tamil to Khmer and Bengali. His idea beat ten other entries, and he only found out he won when he showed up thinking it was just a shortlist meeting. Surprise!

artworks.artworkTitle: The Natural History of Singapore's Mythical Botanic Creatures
artworks.artist: Soh Pei Ling; Chan Mei Hsien; Long Yinghan
artworks.description:
Fantastical bronze creatures make their home in the station’s nooks and crannies. They serve as a whimsical reminder of the wildlife that populate the Bukit Timah Nature Reserve located near the station, as well as the juxtaposition of nature and the built environment.

Fun Fact:
Keep your eyes peeled — this station is full of bronze creatures with names as quirky as their personalities: Chef Annatto, Squirttertarian, Scissor Wedelia, Luckalonians, and more. Hidden in corners and ledges, each sculpture adds a spark of wonder to your commute.

But there’s more — check out the overhead bridge for playful drawings by students from Methodist Girls’ School! These illustrated “field notes” mix real observations with fictional stories, turning the station into a mini nature walk meets imagination lab.

artworks.description:
The Chinese idiom for resilience (自强不息) dissolves into bands of white and yellow to form a blazing sun that shares its light through thick and thin. The words for gratitude (饮水思源) are presented in blue and green, symbolising a running river that connects us to our history and cultural heritage.

Fun Fact:
This station’s walls are actually made of . . . thousands of handwritten messages! Students, teachers, and alumni from Hwa Chong filled them with stories of kindness, courage, and thankfulness.

If you look closely, you might find a secret message just for you—like a treasure map of good vibes hidden in plain sight!

artworks.description:
Trees presented in different mediums around the station pay tribute to its namesake and Singapore’s widespread greenery. The iconic Tembusu tree appears as a photograph, a metal artwork, and a sandblasted silhouette, accompanied by quotes by poets, artists, and nature lovers found on the station’s floor.

Fun Fact:
There’s a super famous tree hiding inside this station — so famous, it’s even on Singapore’s $5 note! The artist used not one, not two, but three different versions of the same Tembusu tree across the station. One even shows bugs crawling on it and birds hanging out like it’s a tree party!

And if you look down at the floor, you might spot poems and secret tree messages “fallen” like leaves waiting to be read.

artworks.description:
To reflect the station’s maritime theme and port location, this work features abstract shapes that hint at sea creatures, clouds, trees, ships and footprints. The silkscreened artworks are spread over sixty vitreous enamel wall panels throughout the station, echoing the transience of water, travel, and voyages.

Fun Fact:
The artwork here isn’t just nice to look at—it’s designed to spark your imagination while you’re on the move.

Some people say it feels like the pictures are “moving” with you as you walk past them! That’s because the panels are placed in a way that plays with motion, just like how water and clouds are always shifting. It’s like a mini adventure for your eyes every time you enter or leave the station!

artworks.description:
This contemporary work combines graffitied words from the aspirations of ordinary people and everyday images viewed in an intriguing way. The anamorphic art is only revealed in full when viewed from a certain angle, coming together to form images of surprising detail and depth. 

Fun Fact:
Artist Matthew Ngui’s Point of View at Potong Pasir station invites you to see everyday sights in a whole new way! What seems like random words and images from a distance actually form detailed art when viewed from the right angle.

Even better? The artwork includes real thoughts and aspirations from the local community, making it a one-of-a-kind reflection of the people who live here. Next time you pass by, take a moment to discover the hidden stories within!

artworks.description:
Prints of commuters are etched on zinc plates on the station walls. These offer a candid and casual snapshot of people walking, interacting or going about their day, immortalising this slice of urban life for posterity. 

Fun Fact:
Artist April Ng’s zinc etchings at Woodleigh station may look effortless, but getting them there was anything but. To transfer her photos of everyday commuters onto the plates, she needed photocopies enlarged to precise dimensions — and on acetate tracing paper, no less. Cue a wild hunt across Singapore, visiting countless shops (and even waiting two hours for a single copy!) just to find the perfect one. All that work, so the artwork could capture these fleeting moments with the detail they deserve.

artworks.description:
Woodcut prints rendered in striking primary colours give this work a heart-warming and joyful look back at childhood. The colourful, distinctly textured images of children at play, accompanied by dragonflies, baby birds and puppies, capture the innocence and wonder of childhood. 

Fun Fact:
Serangoon station isn’t just bright and cheerful by chance! Artist Eng Joo Heng used bold primary colours in his woodcut prints to echo the vibrant hues of childhood — and the coloured entrances of the station itself (psst: red, blue, yellow, or green — what’s your entrance?).

To get each print just right, he had to carve thirty separate wood blocks for just ten prints. That’s a whole lot of carving, rolling, and printing — but the result? Pure joy on the walls.

artworks.description:
This work juxtaposes topographical maps from 1945 and the present day, making a commentary on time and progress in the Kovan area. One largely agricultural and the other depicting expressways and new developments, the maps are made from granite and stainless-steel inlays set on the station floor and complemented by cartographic symbols. 

Fun Fact:
Kovan station offers a glimpse into the area’s transformation! Artist Eng Tow’s floor art contrasts two maps: one from Kovan’s agricultural past, and the other from its modern, developed landscape. The shift from lush greenery to urban development is captured in stunning granite and stainless-steel inlays.

Did you know Serangoon Road is said to be named after the ranggong bird, once found near the Serangoon River? A fascinating snapshot of history beneath your feet!

artworks.description:
Hougang station showcases a community art made from the handprints of 3,000 people, from babies and grandmothers to foreign workers and community leaders. Originally imprinted in wet clay, these handprints were later transferred onto the concrete wall and arranged in a seamless sequence. 

Fun Fact:
Among the sea of handprints at Hougang station lies the gentle touch of a 102-year-old, and even the unique imprint of a lady with deformed fingers.

Artist Seck Yok Ying made sure every hand—no matter the age or shape — found its place on the wall. It’s not just art, it’s a quiet tribute to every life that shapes the Hougang community.

artworks.description:
Bold colours and graphic patterns fused with old black-and-white photos of life in Singapore are embellished with dots, graphic motifs, and bands to create an experimental commentary on the vitality of Buangkok New Town. 

Fun Fact:
Look closely at the artworks in Buangkok station and you’ll spot something curious—dots! Artist Vincent Leow layered these onto old black-and-white photos to reflect how we see the world today — through screens, pixels, and layers.

artworks.description:
Stained-glass windows filter light into the station, filling it with coloured rays. The glass murals mark Sengkang’s changes from kampongs to high-rise buildings that are now part of the landscape. Like time passing, the work also changes throughout the day with the transition of the sun across the sky.

Fun Fact:
Sengkang station’s stained-glass murals don’t just shine—they tell a story of change! Artist Koh Bee Liang took inspiration from local stories, gathering insights from longtime residents and priests who shared their memories of the area’s transformation from kampongs to modern high-rises. And as the sun moves through the sky, the light filters through the glass, turning the artwork into a living piece that changes with the time of day.

A poem by Lee Tzu Pheng adds an extra layer of reflection to the journey.

artworks.description:
Intensely hued but with a unique clarity, these representations of water, the seaside, kampongs and trees are painted on layers of glass. Featuring thin layers of hand-painted glass embedded with fragments of materials, the work has a shimmering effect that changes with the viewing angle.

Fun Fact:
Artist Goh Beng Kwan’s artwork at Punggol station draws from his childhood memories of picnics and camping by the beach. The vibrant colours in his work — blue for the ocean, green for the trees — reflect his connection to Punggol’s natural beauty.

He started with ink and coloured paper paintings, which were then translated into layers of paint on glass, creating a dynamic visual experience as you move through the station.

artworks.description:
TRAJECTORIES maps out the past, present and future of Punggol through the eyes and personal experience of the artist. In his formative teenage years, the artist had spent a significant amount of time exploring the ubiquitous tracks and forested areas of Punggol, fishing, fruit hunting, and swimming in the Serangoon River with his neighbourhood friends. Cycling from Hougang to Punggol jetty, the artist has a memory map of the sites and scape of Punggol of yesteryear.

Now, as a resident of Northshore, the artist bears witness to the significant changes in the landscape of Punggol from its past, present and towards its future as a modern dwelling enclave and the Punggol Digital District.

Fun Fact:
Zul Othman’s TRAJECTORIES takes you on a ride through time in Punggol. As a teen, Zul would explore the area, swimming in the Serangoon River and swinging from ropes tied to trees near Punggol Track 20. His artwork captures these memories while reflecting Punggol’s transformation into the digital district we see today. The cyclist in the artwork? That’s based on his wife, artist SPAZ!

artworks.description:
This work captures the journey, nobility, and strength of Singapore’s Chinese forefathers. A richly-coloured phoenix mural adorns the concourse wall, while an accompanying poem written by the artist in three sets of rhyming couplets is presented in granite tiles.

Tan Swie Hian’s "Couplets on Chinatown" reads as follows:

The nubula swirls with a dance around the River South;
The Equator darts through a song towards the sea shore. 

The buildings in harmony do away the random shadows;
The waste land of ruined gravels grows a million flowers.

They set sail in the boundless sea, suddenly losing sight of home and the cloud route led to the Southern Sky. It was a necessity therefore it was possible; believing in their being capable, therefore they were omnipotent: to lift the heavy cauldron, to carry on their back sacks of rice, to open up new land in the barren waste for cultivation. Chapters of history are laden with tales of the labouring coolies:

They are destined to live and die on the small island, as the alien soil has hardened forever and the phoenix’s song fills up the whole city. Striving for survival, and then for excellence; caring for being good, they have done all things better: in building the society, the school, the family and the nation. Roads, lanes and shops in the community miss the sight of the bullock carts.  

Fun Fact:
This artwork is extra special to the artist because he spent three years living in Chinatown in the 1960s! For artist Tan Swie Hian, this was his chance to create something meaningful for the nation on the very soil where his ancestors first arrived in Singapore.

And the phoenix? It represents the unflinching spirit of the early settlers who built Singapore’s future, rising strong and resilient. Can you see the power of the phoenix soaring across the mural as you walk by?

artworks.description:
This work presents the theme of reflections in two ways: through a 60-metre mural that depicts life on the Singapore River and an acid-etched brass panel that calls to mind the sight of light glinting off a water surface. Both pieces celebrate the energy and life that surround the river through which the station runs. 

These artworks are also accompanied by floor tiles of the painted eyes found on tongkangs or Chinese junks, which help guide commuters through the station.

Fun Fact:
Artist Chua Ek Kay once described the Singapore River as having “a life of its own”—and he brought that life into Clarke Quay station using ink on rice paper, later transformed into massive murals. These are among the largest artworks he has ever created! And those glittering brass tiles? There are sixty of them, each one 8 kilograms — about the weight of a small watermelon!

Chua often painted in the quiet of night, from 10pm to 3am, when the city slept, and his creativity flowed like the river itself.

artworks.description:
Wall murals on football, swimming, and athletics exuberantly capture the surrounding area’s sporting past – a stadium and turf club were located here in pre-war days. The black ink brush drawings alternate with paintings of abstract shapes and lines in pastel colours to depict movement, dynamism, and vibrancy.

Fun Fact:
Before the roar of the Sports Hub, Farrer Park was where Singapore’s love for sports truly found its stride. It was home to our very first turf club and is often called the birthplace of Singapore soccer. Artist Poh Siew Wah made sure the spirit lives on — he even worked with architects to place each artwork right where the action used to happen.

So, if you’re entering by the football mural, you’re not just catching a train — you’re stepping onto the same ground where history was made.

Icons of everyday life are woven into colourful, child-like paintings that offer a glimpse of Boon Keng's past and present. Take a closer look at the interactive art scattered around the station to spot whimsical details amid the busy landscapes of figures and scenery.

Fun Fact:
Boon Keng’s vibrant art is like a snapshot of the area’s past and present, brought to life by artist Lim Poh Teck! Inspired by his childhood sketching sessions along the Kallang River, Lim’s playful icons reflect Boon Keng’s transformation, from sawmills to the lively neighbourhood we know today.

Look closely and you’ll spot over thirty unique icons scattered across the station — each one telling a little story from the past. Ready to take a stroll through Boon Keng’s history?

artworks.description:
The artwork brings to light the symbiotic relationship between flora and fauna, which both provide for and feed from our precious earth. This is a reflection of the collaboration between the artist and the community of Canberra, through her re-interpretation of their imagination and sketches of the evolving flora and fauna they encountered while journeying together through Sungei Buloh Wetland Reserve.

Beyond capturing the richness of plant and animal life, the mural manifests how living organisms grow and adapt constantly to the changing environmental landscape caused by man and pollution, like the ebb and flow of the tide.

FunFact:
Before the mural at Canberra station was created, the artist took residents on guided nature walks through Sungei Buloh Wetland Reserve.

Inspired by the flora and fauna they encountered, participants sketched imaginative versions of what they saw — twenty-eight of those drawings were later transformed into the artwork you see today. Each detail in the mural is a reflection of nature reimagined—rooted in the wetlands, shaped by community wonder.

This art piece was originally commissioned by City Developments Limited (CDL) for its flagship 280-metre skyscraper, Republic Plaza at Raffles Place, and is now located at the entrance of Raffles Place station.

Symphony represents the changes in the city over time, resembling a person in motion and celebrates the people who have helped make Singapore a global city. The bottom sphere represents Singapore's past achievements while the sphere above symbolises the country's ambition for the future.

Sculpted in bronze by renowned Spanish artist Rosa Serra, Symphony captures her signature fluid forms to express motion.

artworks.description:
Formed from stories of the community under the broad themes of nature, the environment of the city and nation-building, participants told their stories on slabs of raw clay. The divergent inspirations were then hand-painted by the artist and assembled into a cohesive whole, reflective of the shared aspirations for the country of their originators.

The artwork celebrates Singapore’s tapestry of life, rich in texture, diversity and colour, yet united in aspiration. For more information, click here .

Fun Fact:
To celebrate SG50, nearly 1,500 people came together to shape a mural like no other. Each person shared a story — etched into raw clay, then hand-painted and pieced together by the artist.

Now stretching across the station walls, this mural is a living tapestry of Singapore’s hopes, memories, and dreams—woven from many hands, but speaking with one voice.

artworks.description:
Crossing borders is an essential part of our lives that enables us to encounter new possibilities.

The artist invites individuals to share their experiences doing exactly that. Their stories are placed in the wide-open sky where there are no boundaries. Through this work, the artist seeks to understand how individuals address the conflicting desires of staying within their comfort zones while fulfilling the need to widen their horizons.

The skyline montage comprises photos taken between 2015 and 2018.

Fun Fact:
At this station, two skylines quietly face each other—one from Singapore, one from across the border in Malaysia. But which is which? The artist snapped these real cityscapes between 2015 and 2018, then set them against a wide, open sky. It’s like two cities waving at each other through the clouds.

artworks.description:
A Syllabus For Stevens is inspired by the station’s proximity to several schools as well as the artist’s love of books and wordplay.

Together with students from Raffles Girls’ School, which used to be located nearby, Rao generated a book list in 2015 of whimsical, funny, and wishful subjects for the artwork, featuring subject titles students wish were taught in schools. Mimicking long library-like shelves of books, A Syllabus For Stevens showcases solid "book spines" with engraved titles that allude to unheard-of branches of knowledge. The book spines are overlaid with limestone chalk, such that the materials (chalk on slate) are another pun about education.

The artwork captures the brief, youthful irreverence of schoolgirls, permanently engraved with the gravitas of a stone library.

Fun Fact:
The book spines at Stevens station are made from slate, with each quirky subject title overlaid in limestone chalk — a cheeky twist on old classroom tools. It’s like the wall is made from giant blackboards, but instead of lessons, it’s filled with hilarious make-believe subjects that students wish were real!

artworks.description:
With Napier station located next to Singapore Botanic Gardens—a UNESCO World Heritage Site managed by the National Parks Board (NParks)—this artwork is a tapestry of the Gardens’ rich collections of botanical paintings, which were originally used for research and education.

The pieces are adapted from late 19th and early 20th-century paintings by the artists James and Charles de Alwis. The de Alwis brothers were born into a family of artists in Sri Lanka and were recruited to work as botanical artists in the Gardens by Henry Nicholas Ridley (Director of the Gardens from 1888 to 1912).

The paintings were selected by Christina Soh (a former librarian at the Gardens) and digitally enhanced and composed by NParks' resident designer, Lim Yn Sheao. Originals of the paintings from the Gardens' collections can be viewed at the Botanical Art Gallery in the Gardens' Gallop Extension.

Fun Fact:
Back in 1890, Charles and James de Alwis came to Singapore to paint plants — for real! Charles earned $450 a year, plus $216.65 just for paints, brushes, and erasers. Their detailed botanical art now blooms across the walls of Napier station, adapted from the original paintings you can still see at the Botanical Art Gallery. Who knew train rides could come with a splash of art history?

artworks.description:
This artwork features a network of industrial-scale pipes interposed with gleaming and colourful entry and exit points.

PULSE is a self-contained system that depicts the flow of resources and people (commuters) transferred daily across our urban environment, through infrastructure such as the Mass Rapid Transit (MRT) system.

The artwork looms as a visual metaphor reflecting on these vital systems, like transporting vital nutrients and oxygen, pulsating to keep our body/city alive.

Fun Fact:
At Orchard Boulevard, the colourful pipes tell a hidden story. They form a visual metaphor representing parts of the human body: red for the heart, yellow for synapses, and blue for the brain. It’s a fitting design for a station near Singapore’s medical hub — like a city’s heartbeat, pulsing with people instead of blood!

artworks.artworkTitle: Scotts Road / Orchard Road from ION Sky
artworks.description:
Encounter the lights of Orchard Road as you emerge from Orchard station.

This artwork captures the night lights taken from the vantage point of ION Sky, as a form of cartography (map-making) as well as mark-making. The length of an entire roll of film was used to trace the area as one seamless image, unlike typical frame-by-frame photography. With a panoramic camera and medium format photographic film, the lights were captured layer by layer, with multiple exposures. These render the bird’s-eye view of Orchard Road into abstraction, as if one is looking into the night sky, gazing at the stars.

Fun Fact:
Instead of snapping separate photos, the artist used an entire roll of film to capture Orchard Road as one flowing image, layering lights on top of lights with multiple exposures. That’s why the artwork looks like a sky full of stars — but it’s actually the city glowing from above!

artworks.description:
Advertisements reveal signs of their times. Looking at them takes us back to a different time and space.

Great World, Great Times was inspired by the artist’s parents’ wedding photo taken near Great World in the 1970s. Each photograph is based on an old advertisement but re-enacted with present-day participants, and featuring choral singers, sports wrestlers, an opera performer and a film curator. Viewers are invited to accompany them on this evocative rumination of what the past used to be and what the future might hold.

Logos of Shaw Brothers, Sky, Atlantic and Globe Theatres and the movie poster of Penarek Beca are reproduced with courtesy from The Shaw Organisation.

Fun Fact:
When taking photos for the artwork at Great World station, the artist told one model to “smile seductively” — like a movie star in an old-time ad! She even included her parents’ wedding photo from the 1970s near Great World, blending real family memories with vintage vibes. It’s like stepping into a time-travelling photo album!

artworks.description:
Havelock Traces attempts to recreate the pre-independence buildings of the Havelock precinct. The artwork sheds light on the history of the area, the community that used to exist there, and the impact of urban renewal.

Archival drawings of the "lost" buildings of Havelock Road offer a glimpse into town planning in pre-independence Singapore and how it affected the way we lived and worked. As commuters enter the station from the different entrances, they will encounter fragments of buildings and structures that reveal a glimpse into the past.

Archival drawings courtesy of the Building Control Division collection, National Archives of Singapore. Archival drawings have been modified for the purpose of creating the artwork.

Fun Fact:
Hidden in the walls of Havelock station are pieces of buildings that don’t exist anymore! These bits are based on real blueprints from pre-independence Singapore— so if it feels like you're stepping into the past for a moment, that’s because you kind of are. It’s like a history scavenger hunt . . . built right into the station!

artworks.description:
"Mata" means eye in Malay, and "mata-mata" refers to a policeman. Both terms inspired this community-generated visual map of the area around Outram Park station, which includes the Police Cantonment Complex located nearby. Members of the public were invited to contribute photos of objects in the neighbourhood. The photos were then jointly converted by the contributors and the artist into drawings. The result is a celebration of the different things that make up this community space beyond just geography. What do we see and remember, and how do we connect them to each other?

Fun Fact:
In the artwork at Outram Park station, there’s a tiny surprise tucked next to a lantern — the artist’s autograph! It’s a sneaky little signature you might miss if you're not looking closely. Think of it as the artist’s wink to all sharp-eyed commuters. Can you find it?

artworks.artworkTitle: Old Chinatown In New Maxwell Life
artworks.description:
Inspired by his upbringing in the Chinatown-Maxwell area, the artist hopes to create a series of artworks based on public buildings, people and the rich culture and heritage of Singapore. It is a journey into how Singaporeans ate, played and dressed in the past, with icons such as a hawker food street, lightbulbs, Samsui women, clouds, and a popular Chinese quote about food consumption in everyday life.

Fun Fact:
At Maxwell station, the artwork acts like a hidden set of clues!

Each entrance features symbols that hint at what’s nearby above ground. Hawker lightbulbs and a child eating? That’s the way to Maxwell Food Centre. Deity clouds? You’re heading toward the temple. And if you spot Samsui women, you’re near the HDB flats — a tribute to the women who helped build Singapore, brick by brick.

artworks.artworkTitle: The Day’s Thoughts of a Homespun Journey into Night
artworks.description:
Just as home is a solace for us to dream comfortably, these transport forms feature cosy fabric patterns that inspire us to experience home, even as we travel around. Like a tapestry of the intertwining routes that we take, the vehicle drivers’ state of mind and thoughts are “projected” from the headlights into thought bubbles.

Through this artwork, commuters are offered new insights about the surroundings along the travelling routes we take, day and night.

Fun Fact:
Before this artwork ever took shape, the artist spent time walking through Woodlands back in 2012, snapping photos of everyday sights that locals might pass without a second glance. These glimpses of neighbourhood life were then transformed into dreamy patterns, vehicles, and thought bubbles you’ll now find across the station. It’s like a visual time capsule — part memory, part daydream — waiting to be rediscovered by those who call Woodlands home.

artworks.description:
Taking the form of trompe l’œil murals, the artwork draws attention to everyday spaces easily missed by commuters on the go. It also bridges the old Central Business District (CBD) and the new financial centre in Marina Bay, where this station is located.

The artist created original charcoal drawings which were then scanned and printed onto vitreous enamel panels. The artwork takes reference from the stairways, doors, windows, and interstitial spaces, such as alleys and corridors, found in areas like Cecil Street, Raffles Place, and Shenton Way in the old CBD. The old CBD is transposed into the new financial centre, creating uncanny moments of "unfamiliar" familiarity and invoking memories of an ever-changing city.

Fun Fact:
Believe it or not, the entire artwork at Marina Bay station was drawn using only charcoal — like a giant black-and-white sketchbook stretched across the walls! With nothing but shades of grey, the artist brought old stairways, doors, and alleys to life, proving that sometimes a little charcoal can sketch a whole city's soul.

artworks.description:
This artwork is a further exploration of the artists’ interest in botany and linking it to visual language and history. Shadows of tropical plants are used as furtive images to echo the presence and absence of nature in our urban environment.

Planting Shadows was adapted from botanical drawings produced during the colonial era, tracing the past to the present landmark of Gardens by the Bay, where we see the cultivation and celebration of nature.

Fun Fact:
Look closely at the leafy shadows on the walls — they come from three real tropical plants: pomegranate, known as the fruit of knowledge; ginger, one of Southeast Asia’s oldest herbs; and chempedak, a jackfruit lookalike that you can tell apart just by its shape. These shadowy forms trace a quiet connection between nature, history, and the garden city above.

artworks.description:
These sculptures are imaginings of sound mirrors or acoustic mirrors, a form of proto-radar developed in the early 20th century that was used to listen to the skies as a form of surveillance during World War I. Numerous designs and methods of operation were born across the world.

In the 1930s, it was claimed that there were plans to construct and install five to eight sound mirrors around Singapore by the British. However, these plans never came to fruition, as the biophonic activity – sounds emanating from wildlife in natural settings – proved too loud and disruptive for the effectiveness of these surveillance mechanisms. The construction plan thus became an unheard colonial history.

The artwork envisions the area of Tanjong Rhu as one of the places where these sound mirrors could have been installed.

Fun Fact:
At Tanjong Rhu station, you'll find two intriguing sculptures: one shaped like a honeycomb, the other like plant cells. These aren't just artistic designs — they're inspired by the concept of acoustic mirrors, devices once considered for detecting enemy aircraft. While such mirrors were never built in Singapore due to our vibrant natural soundscape, these sculptures serve as a playful nod to that history, reminding us that sometimes, nature's own sounds can be the loudest of all!​

artworks.artworkTitle: “The waters are blue; Yet I pine for you”
artworks.description:
The mural combines memories and complex histories of Singapore’s reclaimed eastern coast, where this station sits. Using archival images, family photos (including Sim’s own), and pictures she’s taken, the collage contains vignettes of the area’s past. From the iconic Big Splash, local swimming clubs with pagars (enclosures) that were once in the sea, Kampong Amber – home to Malay fishermen and Peranakan communities, and the now-endangered Katong (Javanese for leatherback sea turtle) that once came ashore at the tanjong (cape).

With a whimsical, hand-cut aesthetic, the mural makes speculation about whose narratives are hidden in history.

This artwork’s title is taken from the first stanza of the popular folk song "Di Tanjung Katong".

Fun Fact:
Tanjong Katong station’s mural is packed with memories — if you look closely, you’ll even spot a photo of the artist and her mother tucked into the collage! The artwork also gives a nod to the iconic Big Splash water park, once a family favourite until 2016.

And here’s a wild one: “Katong” comes from the Javanese word for leatherback sea turtle, which used to crawl ashore right here. Who knew a single mural could hold so many waves of history?
