artworks.description:
WALK encapsulates the essence of fluid tranquillity and extends its company to those who pass by while making their daily journeys. Crafted to look like it is from a single block of plasticine, the sculpture preserves subtle imprints from its packaging. Delicate traces are left by the artist’s fingertips and her childhood memory of creating plasticine sculptures with her sisters during school vacations.

Amidst the perpetual rhythm of the cityscape, the artwork offers a moment of tactile reflection and serves as a nod to the shared pilgrimage of city dwellers.

The sculpture thus emerges not merely as a static creation but as a dynamic narrative that serves as a symbol of life’s journey, paying tribute to the people who have walked through life and contributed to the tapestry of Marine Parade’s rich history.

Fun Fact:
The sculpture at Marine Parade might remind you of your art class — and that’s the point! The artist used real plasticine as a model and even sent a block of it to the fabricators so they could match the colour and texture exactly. Those soft-looking curves and fingerprints? They're a tribute to childhood creativity and all the quiet, thoughtful walks that have shaped the Marine Parade story.

artworks.artworkTitle: A seat at the end of the long, long, long table
artworks.description:
A seat at the end of the long, long, long table is an exploration of family lines, histories, roots, and ties across generations and geographical zones. Illustrated as a family tree, the artist collaborated with families from the Marine Terrace area to create an artwork that establishes familial ties that are reimagined as star constellations, taking reference from family portraits and photographs.

Fun Fact:
For centuries, family trees have helped people around the world trace their roots and honour their heritage. At Marine Terrace station, this tradition takes on a celestial twist — the artwork reimagines family trees as constellations, where each person, past or present, shines like a star in the shared sky of memory. It’s a quiet tribute to the invisible threads that connect us across time.

artworks.description:
It wasn’t so long ago when the sea would have been above you, and no one had yet named this place Bayshore. In the time to come, perhaps the shoreline will be elsewhere once again, and the very sense of what this place is will continue to evolve.

Farther Shores takes images of our Bayshore as it is and blends them with other places that share the same name. It also shares about the imagined possibilities of what Bayshore may once have been and what it may yet be. This is not to disorient us in time and space, but to broaden our sense of who we are, what we have been, and what we may become.

Fun Fact:
Not long ago, this very spot was part of the sea! Bayshore stands on reclaimed land—a shining example of Singapore’s coastal transformation. And the artwork here takes it a step further: the photographs are made in the style of cyanotype, a 19th-century technique that turns images a deep, oceanic blue. It's a tribute to both the history of the coastline and the ever-changing idea of “shore”.

artworks.description:
3652 x 50 is a time capsule containing fifty promises made by participants who live, work or study around the Woodlands South station. These promises are made to themselves about what they intend to achieve in ten years’ time. The project began in 2013 in anticipation that by the time the station opened, the participants would have entered different chapters of their lives. The daily commute may be seen as merely getting from point A to B, but it can also be a journey of life in which we can choose how we want it to unfold.

Fun Fact:
This station’s walls were already set to feature a Penrose tiling pattern — a design so clever, it never repeats. While studying it, the artist noticed little stars hiding in the pattern. That sparked an idea: what if those stars grew into a “Garden City”, where fifty handwritten promises could quietly bloom? Now they do — tucked between colours and geometry like tiny hopes in full disguise.

artworks.description:
The main feature of the artwork is the banyan tree, juxtaposed with buildings, imaginatively interpreted characters, and artefacts taken from the unique blend of nostalgic shophouses and lush greenery around Springleaf estate. This makes the artwork a study of the integration of the place, people, nature, and the spatial connection between them. The artwork is a whimsical composition that invites the busy commuter to wander into a familiar yet enchanted, magical world amidst their transit.

Fun Fact:
Did you know the artwork at Springleaf hides little surprises like a treasure map? Look closely and you might spot a childhood paratrooper toy, a pineapple-shaped lantern, and even the Chinese symbols for the sun (日) and moon (月) hiding in the branches! It’s like a jungle where stories grow on trees!

artworks.description:
Interlude for Lentor takes its inspiration from the area around the station, and in particular, Teachers’ Estate, where the roads are named after famous poets and philosophers. Inspired by the meaning and the location, the artist created a visual score that resembles musical notations and calligraphic scripts.

Commuters are invited to read the visual score as they traverse the station, to enjoy an interlude amidst their busy day.

Fun Fact:
If the wall at Lentor station looks like it's moving . . . you're not imagining things! The artist used a visual trick called a moiré pattern—kind of like an optical illusion magic. It’s made from fabric, not tech, but it still wiggles and dances as you walk past. Try it . . . take a few steps and watch the artwork groove with you!

artworks.description:
This series of twenty-two bird sculptures is inspired by the thriving songbird-watching community in the neighbourhood. Artist Song-Ming Ang’s bird sculptures are scattered throughout the seven station entrances and train platforms, portraying that the birds have found their way into the station by accident.

The seven species represented in Ang’s sculptures are the most common birds kept as pets in Singapore and Southeast Asia. They include the Mata Puteh (Oriental White-eye), Jambul (Red-whiskered Bulbul), Merbok (Zebra Dove), Huamei (Laughing Thrush), White-rumped Shama (Magpie Robin), Domestic Canary, and Blue Flycatcher.

Fun Fact:
Just around the corner from Mayflower station is the Kebun Baru Birdsinging Club — Singapore’s largest bird-singing arena! On weekend mornings, over 1,000 birdcages hang high on poles as proud bird owners cheer on their chirpy stars. The twenty-two bird sculptures at the station? They’re inspired by these feathered performers who make this part of town sing!

artworks.description:
Artist anGie’s art is inﬂuenced by her childhood memories of growing up in Bright Hill, playing around the neighbourhood and the nearby Bishan Park, which became the main form of the artwork.

In line with the nature-inspired station design, the artwork features microscopic details of forms, colours, lines and networks of plants found in Bishan Park. These reﬂects the bonds developed within the communities, like the network found in nature.

The artwork also features motifs of “reﬂections” on the ﬂoor, which — together with the bright artwork on the lift shaft — depict the idea of light and shadow from Bright Hill.

Fun Fact:
The colourful artwork on the lift shaft isn’t just decoration — it’s actually shaped like Bishan Park 1, as if you're looking at it from way up in the sky! And here’s a secret: the artist still takes the MRT and often rides through this station. So, if you see someone grinning at the wall . . . you might be riding with the artist herself!

artworks.description:
With the largest nature reserve on the island just a short distance away, this neighbourhood is no stranger to curious visitors from all walks of life. Sometimes, these include the indigenous residents of the long-tailed variety. And it seems like a number of them have gotten lost in this station. Could you spare some time to show these animals the way home if you happen to ﬁnd them? They would greatly appreciate it!

Fun Fact:
There are eighty-eight cheeky animals hiding all around Upper Thomson station! They’ve wandered in from the nearby nature reserve and now they’re peeking from walls, ceilings, and corners. Keep your eyes peeled—it's like playing hide-and-seek with animals in a concrete jungle!

artworks.description:
:) (: transforms the station’s walls into a screen which greets us by saying "HELLO :)" when we arrive and depart. The station walls appear to be grinning at us, presenting a static performance of dancing strings with smileys. They absorb murmurs through the station’s acoustic panels, while emitting a silent message for artwork audiences or simply passengers.

The artwork transforms the daily commuters into fascinating characters as they transit to and from the station, carrying ﬂows of energy and giving meaning to the station.

Fun Fact:
At Caldecott station, the smiley-faced walls aren’t just cute — they’re clever! Artist Claire Lim worked with Professor Stylianos Dritsas, a specialist in design computation, to create the artwork using software that transformed her drawings into thousands of tiny holes.

But here’s the cool part: every hole was tweaked ever so slightly based on advice from an acoustics engineer to help absorb sound. It’s like a giant emoji that also moonlights as a sound sponge — and it was engineered to do just that!

artworks.description:
Just as rain brings cool relief and washes the city anew, this collection of images brings a refreshing element of nature into the underground station. The fourteen raindrop photos depict scenes that commuters can see if they looked out of the window during a rainy train ride above ground.

Fun Fact:
Artist Nah Yong En took hundreds of photos from train windows on rainy days — but not all of them made the cut! For weeks, he kept his camera ready, rushing to above-ground stations every time it rained. He had to battle dirty windows, blurry drops, and tricky lighting — all for that perfect shot.

In the end, only fourteen photos were chosen and placed on opposite sides of the platform to show two moods: one side is “greenery”, the other, “blue evenings".

artworks.description:
The mobile of delicate sculptures resembling flowers was originally hung in Orchard station. It was re-commissioned as homage to the legacy of art in Singapore’s MRT train stations, from before the Art in Transit programme was launched in 1997 for the North East Line.

Fun Fact:
This floating bouquet actually started its life hanging above the ticket gates at Orchard station back in the 1980s! The new version is bigger than the original, stretches 10 metres long, and gently moves in the breeze between two train lines. Tay Chee Toh called it a “blessing in disguise” to see it come to life again in the heart of the city.

artworks.description:
The energetic history of a bustling port is depicted in a series of watercolour paintings, which imagine what Sir Stamford Raffles might have seen from his house on Fort Canning Hill.

Fun Fact:
Lim Tze Peng was Singapore’s oldest artist — and he was still painting past the age of 100! He passed away in 2025, at the remarkable age of 103, leaving behind a lifetime of art.

This station showcases his memories of the old Singapore River, filled with bumboats, shophouses, and kampong life. He used to sketch on-site with a board tucked under one arm, just watching the world go by. Now his paintings travel with you — like postcards from the past in motion.

artworks.description:
Donated by: CapitaLand Development
Year: 2024

Another Day (The Coolies) immortalises the coolie, the archetype of the labourer of bygone days. Coolies were bonded workmen in colonial Singapore, and many laboured at the Singapore River waterfront where trade was brisk and relentless. Expertly balancing heavy loads upon their bare backs and shoulders, they clambered barefoot across wooden gangplanks ferrying cargo from the bumboats to quayside warehouses under the scorching tropical sun.

Another Day shows two coolies, lean and muscular, sharing a simple meal before beginning another day of hard work. One squats comfortably on his wooden stool while the other draws up a leg to his chest as he raises the bowl to his lips.

Chong Fah Cheong draws inspiration from his Singaporean heritage and reflects on his carefree childhood days through these iconic sculptures.

artworks.description:
The bold colours and graphic elements in this series of paintings are inspired by the surrounding area’s cultural heritage. One of the artworks in this series features a collage of symbols related to Chinese opera, law or medicine which represent visual memories of Outram Park.

Fun Fact:
Artist Wang Lu Sheng didn’t just imagine this artwork — he lived it! He spent weekends exploring Outram Park, even climbing to the rooftops of shophouses and apartments to get a bird's-eye view of the neighbourhood. That’s how he collected the memories that inspired his artwork, trying to capture the heart and soul of the area.

It’s like he turned the station into a time capsule, filled with cherished memories from the people who live and work in Outram Park!

artworks.description:
Emerging from and melting into the walls of the station, sixty-nine reliefs of surreal human forms accompany commuters as they make their way through it.

Intended to represent the commuters’ state of mind, these art pieces offer snippets of movement and form, inviting viewers to fill in the blanks. The buildings, landmarks, and objects featured in the artwork also serve as a form of wayfinding for the commuters.

Fun Fact:
Imagine this: every single figure you see on the walls took hours of careful carving — artist Teo Eng Seng worked six days a week for nine months to bring them to life! But here’s the twist: the blank spaces between the figures are on purpose — they’re meant to make you think, like a mini puzzle for your imagination!

And that’s not all — if you look closely, you’ll find little surprises tied to Outram Park’s history, like a Peranakan house outline and even a dragon! Can you spot them?

artworks.description:
As the title suggests, this large-scale artwork represents the interchange between the station lines that run through Dhoby Ghaut. A marriage of ceramic and mosaic works that span walls, climb columns, and sprawl over the station floor, they draw upon the cultural richness and artistic heritage of Singapore and the region, as well as ideas of permanent movement.

Fun Fact:
At first glance, the artwork at Dhoby Ghaut might seem like two separate styles — but look closer! One end begins quietly in soft porcelain blues (Delia’s signature), the other in natural stone mosaics (Milenko’s touch).

As you walk along the mural, something magical happens: their styles begin to blend — growing louder in colour, shape, and energy until they meet in the middle in a joyful swirl! Think of it as a grand artistic duet, where ceramics and mosaics twirl into each other in a dance inspired by Peranakan tiles and Balinese fabrics.

artworks.description:
Spread across the station are some 180 pieces of art tiled into the floors and columns. Featuring various sets of prehistoric symbols such as hunters, fish, and birds, acting as wayfinding icons which lead the commuters towards the columns that signify each route of the station.

These symbols also come together to form a large floor mural and vibrant mosaic wall panel at the main concourse.

Fun Fact:
Artist Sun Yu-Li found his creative rhythm not in a studio, but in a café, sketching for hours with a cup of coffee in hand. Drawing from his love for architecture and ancient symbols, he created a visual “language” made of dots, lines, and shapes inspired by prehistoric art.

Look closely and you’ll spot dancers, hunters, birds and more — each one helping guide commuters through the station. Who knew wayfinding could feel like decoding a secret message from the past?

artworks.description:
This series of evocative, sepia-toned works celebrates Indian culture through traditional Indian folk-art forms and elaborate bronze and granite floor works. Stylised animal paintings adorn the walls, alluding to the buffalo stables that gave the surrounding area its local name. 

Fun Fact:
If you’ve ever spotted the elegant floor patterns at Little India station and thought they looked like they belonged in a home, you're not far off. Artist S. Chandrasekaran took inspiration from Kolam designs he saw growing up, when his mother would draw them at the doorstep each morning. These aren’t exact replicas, but they carry that same energy of new beginnings and quiet devotion.

Oh, and the buffalo? That’s a nod to the station’s old nickname— Kandang Kerbau (aka buffalo pens)! Yes, there’s a whole lot of heart beneath your feet.

artworks.artworkTitle: Seeing History Through the Bamboo Forest
artworks.description:
In Seeing History Through the Bamboo Forest , artist Yeo Siak Goon masterfully weaves together the rich tapestry of Little India’s heritage in his signature style and colours.

Inspired by Little India’s colloquial Hokkien name, "Tek Kah" — meaning 'at the foot of the bamboos'— he artist places a screen of bamboos prominently in the foreground, through which glimpses of colonial-era life are revealed. Many of these scenes are transport-related — a rickshaw puller, trishaw rider, bullpen, bull-carts, and a 1950s public bus, evoking the rapid evolution of transportation in Singapore over the decades.

In the background, a vibrant panoramic collage of familiar landmarks anchors the composition: KK Women’s and Children’s Hospital, Tekka Centre, shophouses, a Hindu temple, and the Land Transport Authority’s Hampshire Office.

The artwork beautifully juxtaposes the past and the present in contrasting tones of monochrome and vivid colour, symbolising continuity across time–from past to present; fitting homage as the Land Transport Authority celebrates its 30th anniversary, with 27 of those years spent at the Hampshire Office

artworks.description:
Shenton Way is the financial hub of Singapore. The artwork juxtaposes the daily activities of the people here with our old currencies. People looking up at the Foreign Exchange Index are likened to bird watchers in the park. The Lau Pa Sat lunch crowd is a daily scene depicted, too. People queuing at the ATM and ticket machines can see themselves mirrored in the murals, along with a humorous take on lunchtime joggers who are seen "running" after a banknote (junk boat series).

Many of the models in the paintings are the artist’s own family and friends. Where art imitates life, Everyday Singapore每日所见 is a reflection of ourselves.

Fun Fact:
This artwork wasn’t made on a computer — it was hand-painted on delicate rice paper before being scanned and printed onto the station walls! If you look closely, you might spot the artist’s family, friends, or even pets hiding in the crowd. It’s a financial district scene, but with a little love from everyday life!

artworks.description:
Donated by: PSA International
Year: 2022

The Bo-men transcend race and nationality, connected by the common thread of their shared humanity. Generally displayed in groups, through form and posture they convey a sense of being "alongside" each other, by striving together towards a shared goal. The sculptures celebrate human potential, resilience, and the spirit of collaboration, which echo themes central to the core ethos shared by PSA and Singapore.

Daisy Boman is a world-renowned artist who creates unique pieces that pay tribute to the shared bonds between individuals and their potential to create movements of purpose and passion towards a shared vision.

artworks.description:
Donated by: Kohlberg Kravis Roberts & Co
Year: 2026

Sun Woman captivates the viewer with its joyful and vibrant aesthetic, using childlike shapes and forms to create an instant connection with people of all ages and backgrounds. The iridescent hues seemingly shimmer and change, adding depth and intrigue that invite personal interpretation. At first glance, the exaggerated head with two faces and expressive hands feel light-hearted and playful, yet the artwork holds enough complexity for the viewer to uncover something unique and meaningful with each viewing. It is both an immediate experience and a lasting one, offering a timeless appeal that resonates with both the individual and the collective.

artworks.description:
Kampong Siglap is believed to have been founded between 1819 and 1822 by Tok Lasam from the city of Pontianak in modern-day Indonesia. The name "Siglap", derived from the Malay word "gelap", which means darkness that conceals, may be traced to a 19th-century solar eclipse that occurred in tandem with his arrival by sea under the cover of darkness.

The artwork references the method by which the terrain of the moon is studied, utilising mapping data to show the moon’s uneven surface, the play of light and shadows, and transmuting these shadows into perforations on the metal plates. Just as the moon reflects sunlight, the reflective metal mirrors this phenomenon, providing an illusion of iridescence.

Thus, darkness is reconsidered, not as concealing but as the revealing of information and fragmented stories of Tok Lasam’s arrival.

Fun Fact:
Siglap’s name comes from the Malay word "gelap", meaning “darkness”—a nod to a solar eclipse said to have shrouded the arrival of Tok Lasam by sea in the 1800s. And guess what? His final resting place is just a short walk from this station.

The artwork reflects this hidden past with moon-like textures and glowing metal—proof that sometimes, darkness doesn’t hide the truth . . . it helps us find it.

artworks.artworkTitle: Journey Across Sunlight: A Siglap Mosaic
artworks.description:
Donated by: Lim Eng Chong
Year: 2024

Journey Across Sunlight: A Siglap Mosaic is an artwork commissioned by Lim Eng Chong and other Old Victorians (an endearing term for alumni from Victoria School and Victoria Junior College) for Victoria School and the wider Siglap community. Victoria School has nurtured numerous luminaries in the cultural and artistic fields, many of whom are spotlighted in Journey .

Designed by award-winning artist Brian Gothong Tan, himself an Old Victorian, this glass mosaic artwork was inspired by and features elements from the works of Old Victorian luminaries and Singapore Cultural Medallion recipients Dr Iskandar Jalil’s ceramics and Professor Edwin Thumboo’s poetry.

“Iskandar blue”, a tone of blue commonly featured in Iskandar Jalil’s internationally acclaimed ceramics, fills the background of the artwork upon which various motifs and a fragment of poetry by Edwin Thumboo relating to the school and Siglap are layered.

"Victoria at Siglap"by Edwin Thumboo reads:
Across the sunlight in the straits,
three generations knew
That when you move, you take us all with you.

Indeed, through Journey Across Sunlight: A Siglap Mosaic , the Old Victorians move and take forward with them the current and future students and the Siglap community.
